request.question: A-ha was founded by the keyboardest who was of what nationality?

title: Order of Fidelity
Magne Furuholmen
George Willcox
Federation of Gay Games
A-ha
International Boy Scouts, Troop 1
Liberian nationality law
List of A-ha tours
Mintohren
ECUARUNARI
sentences: The Order of Fidelity (Urdhëri Besa), also known as Order of Besa, was founded by King Zog I when he was President of Albania.
Founded on 22 December 1926, it was initially awarded in four classes (1.
Grand Cordon with star-Kordon i Madh me Yll 2.
Grand Officer-Oficer i Madh, 3.
Commander-Komandar, and 4.
Knight-Kalorës) and a medal, and was limited to six ordinary recipients of Albanian nationality, and not awardable to foreigners.
It was remodelled in 1932 and reduced to three classes (1.
Grand Cordon with star-Kordon i Madh me Yll 2.
Commander-Komandar, and 3.
Knight-Kalorës) and remained to be limited to only six ordinary recipients of Albanian nationality, and not made awardable to foreigners,
Magne Furuholmen (born 1 November 1962) is a Norwegian musician and visual artist.

Furuholmen, better known to music fans by his stage name Mags, is the keyboardist of the synthpop/rock band A-ha, and co-wrote many of the band's hits including "Take on Me", "Stay on These Roads", "Manhattan Skyline", "Cry Wolf", "Forever Not Yours", "Analogue (All I Want)", and "Foot of the Mountain".
George Willcox (born 1949) is an archaeobotanist who specialises in the origins of agriculture in the Near East.
He has been employed as researcher with the CNRS (Centre national de la recherche scientifique)France since 1989.
He has dual French and British nationality.

He studied environmental archaeology under the late Prof. Dimbleby at the Institute of Archaeology in London during the early 1970s and was then was employed as environmental archaeologist by the Museum of London.
In 1977 he married and left his job to follow his interest in Near Eastern archaeobotany.
An interest which he had acquired as an undergraduate working with Gordon Hillman in Turkey.

During the next few years he worked in Turkey, Afghanistan Jordan, Oman, Kuwait, Tajikistan, India and Syria and started amassing a charred archaeobotanical collection and modern reference collection.
These are now housed at the Muséum national d’Histoire naturelle in Paris.
It was during this somewhat precarious period that he moved to France and founded a family.

In 1984 Gordon Hillman introduced George to Jacques Cauvin and Patrica Anderson at the CNRS Institut de Préhistore Orientale in south east France where they developed a project on experimentally cultivating wild cereals In 1989 George entered the CNRS working on a number of early Neolithic sites in Syria in particular with Danielle Stordeur at Jerf el Amhar.
He lectured at several French universities on archaeobotany and the origins of agriculture and supervised post graduate students.
He was an associate editor for Vegetation History and Archaeobotany for many years and received an honorary doctorate from the University of Basel in 2013.
His 117 scholarly publications are a measure of his research achievements.

The Federation of Gay Games (FGG) is the sanctioning body of the Gay Games which was founded in 1982 by Tom Waddell, after he dealt first-hand with the prejudice towards gay athletes and their inability to openly participate in sporting events.
The Federation of Gay Games hosts the world's biggest cultural and sporting event for the LGBT community every four years.

The Federation of Gay Games is founded on the principles of "participation, inclusion, and personal best" and continues to support the LGBT community not only through the Gay Games but through scholarships for underprivileged members.
The Federation of Gay Games continues to expand throughout the world and accepts members of any sexual orientation and nationality to participate in the games or contribute.
The FGG is proudly hosting the next Gay Games in 2018 in Paris, France.
A-ha (usually stylized as a"-h"a; ] ) is a Norwegian band formed in Oslo in 1982.
The band was founded by Morten Harket (vocals), Magne Furuholmen (keyboards) and Pål Waaktaar-Savoy (guitars).
The group rose to fame during the mid-1980s after being discovered by musician and producer John Ratcliff, and continued global success in the 1990s and 2000s.

International Boy Scouts, Troop 1 was founded in 1911 as Japan's first Scout troop and, despite its early multinational character, was originally registered with the London headquarters of the Boy Scouts as "British Scouts in Foreign Countries", which was the only option at the time due to the Boy Scouts' "nationality requirement" and no international Boy Scout office.
The troop's character, and Scoutmaster, changed and it was recognized as a mixed-nationality, or "international" troop in 1918.
After formation of the World Scout Bureau, Troop 1 became the first "mixed nationality" group to be registered directly be the Bureau.
The troop has been continuously active since its foundation.

The troop's "mixed nationality" charter dated October 30, 1925 was issued after the newly formed International Bureau received approval to directly register such "international" groups at the 3rd World Scout Conference.
The charter was signed by Lord Baden-Powell.
The Republic of Liberia was founded by African slaves from North America by the American Colonization Society and returned to establish a republic on African soil.
Nationality law is given in the Aliens and Nationality Law of 1973, based on its 1847 Constitution.
Human rights activists have described current citizenship laws as explicitly racist, as it requires being black as a prerequisite to citizenship.
The first constitution allowed for women to transmit their nationality to their children, although multiple citizenship was not permitted nor is it permitted in revisions of the constitution.
A-ha is a Norwegian synthpop act, founded in 1982 by Morten Harket, Magne Furuholmen and Paul Waaktaar-Savoy in Oslo.
Since 1985, A-ha has performed throughout the world, including tours in North America, South America, Europe, Australia, Japan, and China.

Mintohren, also Mintōren, is a grass-roots movement created by young Zainichi Koreans to combat ethnic discrimination by encouraging a multicultural and positive approach to integration of Koreans into Japanese society.

The movement started in the 1970s as a joint effort of three groups: Mukuge no Kai, founded by Lee Kyung-jae in 1972 in Osaka, Tokkabi Kodomo Kai, founded in 1974 in Osaka, and Seikyu Sha, founded in 1974 in Tokyo.

In 1985, they began a campaign in Takatsuki, Osaka Prefecture which resulted in the establishment of a division in the city board of education devoted exclusively to education for ethnic Koreans, and the elimination of the requirement that city employees hold Japanese nationality.

They also played an important role in the 1980s struggle against the requirement that all foreign residents, including Special Permanent Residents like the Zainichi Koreans, give their fingerprints to police; they organised refusers from different parts of Japan, as well as providing information and increasing awareness of their campaign.

ECUARUNARI (in Kichwa: "Ecuador Runakunapak Rikcharimuy ", "Movement of the indigenous people of Ecuador"), also known as Confederation of Peoples of Kichwa Nationality ("Ecuador Kichwa Llaktakunapak Jatun Tantanakuy ," in Spanish "Confederación de Pueblos de la Nacionalidad Kichwa del Ecuador ") is the organization of indigenous peoples of Kichwa nationality in the Ecuadorian central mountain region, founded in 1972.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: A-ha was founded by the keyboardest who was of what nationality?
hop1_docs: Chilean National Zoo | The Chilean National Zoo ("Zoológico Nacional de Chile") is a 4.8 ha zoo that was founded in 1925 in Santiago, Chile.  Located at the foot of San Cristóbal Hill in what is known as the Santiago Metropolitan Park ("Parque Metropolitano de Santiago"), the zoo is home to thousands of animals representing 158 species.  Unique exhibits feature Chilean native animals and birds including rare and endangered species.

Big Gus, What's the Fuss? | Big Gus, What's the Fuss?  (also known as Ha-Balash Ha'Amitz Shvartz and Fat Spy) was a 1973 Israeli comedy film directed by Ami Artzi and American director Lloyd Kaufman, the president of Troma Entertainment.  The plot revolves around a Hebrew detective named Gus and his partner Harry who are hired by a woman to investigate a possible case of adultery.

Hamra National Park | Hamra National Park (Swedish: "Hamra nationalpark" ) is a national park in Ljusdal Municipality, Gävleborg County, Sweden.  The park, which is a part of Orsa Finnmark (part of Dalarna), was founded in 1909 and was then 28 ha in area, but was extended in 2011 to 1383 ha .

Ha Hee-ra | Ha Hee-ra (born October 30, 1969) is a South Korean actress.  She was born in Seoul to a Taiwanese immigrant father and a Korean mother.  Ha began her acting career in 1981 when she was in the sixth grade, appearing mostly in films for teenagers.  In the 1990s, Ha became one of the top actresses in Korean television, along with Chae Shi-ra and Kim Hee-ae.  Among her notable dramas include "What Women Want" (1990), "What Is Love" (1991), "The Break of Dawn" (1993), "Catching Up with Gangnam Moms" (2007) and "President" (2010).

Murrumbidgee Valley National Park | The Murrumbidgee Valley National Park is a protected national park that is located in the Riverina region of New South Wales, in eastern Australia.  The 47703 ha national park is located approximately 6 km east of Hay , and approximately 15 km west of Narrandera .  The park protects part of what is now the largest continuous tract of river red gum forest in the world.

Dark Blue Dream | Dark Blue Dream is the second studio album by composer and keyboardest Steve Weingart.  Produced by Weingart, the album features guitarist Frank Gambale, bassist Jimmy Earl and drummer Tom Brechtlein.  The album was released worldwide in 2006 by Skeewa Music.

Isomgosiono | Isomgosiono, "Queen Anne", 1703 Treaty Signer for Pan NA Federation along with Sachem George Skipper of the Nottoway/Chero-en-ha-ka/Iroquois.  These pan Native American communities go back to what may be communities formed per the one reference on the Capt John Smith Map of 1656 listing a single Shawnee group in the Susquahanna Valley when he mapped the area at that time.  This map is found in the appendix of the Capt. John Smith Trail of the National Colonial Park System.  The parent of Queen Anne is of the Shawnee of that mapped valley and they had been brought over by Pardo in 1570, according to carbon dating of their settlement area (Langston-MOretti, National Park Service, Ch.  4, Archaeology Study ) along with the Old Cheraw, after Pardo conquered the 20,000 Wichita, Kansas Iowans who lived there.  Of those who were left, some were enslaved as "Monocan" (means Hemp Grower in Chiwere) and brought to the Susquahanna. (NPS Langston-Moretti, Ch.  4 and 10 of Weromicococo State Park/National Colonial Park Archaeology Study).  The Cheraw were listed as Xualla by Pardo, Sara by the Tazewell, TN group found by Pardo, and Charra by cartographers of the blended 1724 encampment of surviving small pox plaqued tribes who had to put aside their differences who would not normally have lived near each other.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage [6] notes a "keyboardest" (Steve Weingart) ("Dark Blue Dream", passage [6]). Bridge / missing fact: the keyboardist of the band A-ha (identity and his nationality are unresolved in the passages). Retrieval direction: 1) open the "A-ha" Wikipedia page and inspect the Members/History or Formation section to find the keyboardist's full name; 2) open that keyboardist's biography page (e.g., "Magne Furuholmen") and check the lead/infobox for nationality (search template if needed: site:wikipedia.org "A-ha" "keyboardist" or "Magne Furuholmen nationality"). If you mean a different "A-ha" (not the Norwegian band), please clarify.
hop2_docs: Magne Furuholmen | Magne Furuholmen (born 1 November 1962) is a Norwegian musician and visual artist.  Furuholmen, better known to music fans by his stage name Mags, is the keyboardist of the synthpop/rock band A-ha, and co-wrote many of the band's hits including "Take on Me", "Stay on These Roads", "Manhattan Skyline", "Cry Wolf", "Forever Not Yours", "Analogue (All I Want)", and "Foot of the Mountain".

Past Perfect Future Tense | Past Perfect Future Tense is the first solo album of Norwegian band a-ha's Magne Furuholmen as Magne F. Magne worked in this album with Coldplay members, Will Champion and Guy Berryman, also Travis member, Andy Dunlop.
A Dot of Black in the Blue of Your Bliss | A Dot Of Black In The Blue Of Your Bliss is the second solo album of Norwegian band a-ha's Magne Furuholmen as Magne F.

Martin Halla | Martin Halla (born 4 June 1988 in Aurskog, Norway) is a Norwegian singer who, on 25 May 2012, won the inaugural series of "The Voice – Norges beste stemme", the Norwegian version of "The Voice" in 2012 and was broadcast on TV2.  He was part of "Team Magne" as he was mentored by Magne Furuholmen.
I Call Your Name (A-ha song) | "I Call Your Name" is a song by A-ha.  The second single from their album "East of the Sun, West of the Moon" was written by Paul Waaktaar Savoy and Magne Furuholmen.

List of A-ha tours | A-ha is a Norwegian synthpop act, founded in 1982 by Morten Harket, Magne Furuholmen and Paul Waaktaar-Savoy in Oslo.  Since 1985, A-ha has performed throughout the world, including tours in North America, South America, Europe, Australia, Japan, and China.

Apparatjik | Apparatjik ( ) are a multi-national band formed in 2008.  The band is a supergroup that consists of bassist Guy Berryman from Coldplay, guitarist/keyboardist Magne Furuholmen from A-ha, singer/guitarist Jonas Bjerre of Mew and drummer/producer Martin Terefe.  The band's name literal translation is "agent of the apparatus".  Apparatjik is based on apparatchik, which is a word of Russian origin and is used to describe "an official or bureaucrat in any organization" but has in the past had other meanings, including "Communist agent or spy".
hop2_titles: A Dot of Black in the Blue of Your Bliss
response.summary_2: Claim: A-ha was founded by the band's keyboardist (Magne Furuholmen). This is explicitly supported by 'List of A-ha tours' ([6]) which states A-ha was “founded in 1982 by Morten Harket, Magne Furuholmen and Paul Waaktaar-Savoy,” and by 'Magne Furuholmen' ([1]) which identifies him as a Norwegian musician and “the keyboardist” of A-ha. Passages [2] and [3] note his solo albums, [5] shows he co-wrote A-ha songs, [7] lists him as guitarist/keyboardist in Apparatjik, and [4] also references his Norwegian background; thus [6] and [1] explicitly support the founding-by-keyboardist claim and the others supply metadata. The keyboardist’s nationality (Norwegian) is stated in passage [1].
